title: Compartment Syndrome
oneLiner: Acute limb-threatening ischaemia due to raised pressure in a closed fascial compartment; most common after fracture, requires emergency fasciotomy.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Rhabdomyolysis → acute kidney injury (AKI)**  
  - Single finding: dark/tea-coloured urine (myoglobinuria) + rising potassium >6.0 mmol/L on VBG  
  - This is a medical emergency: aggressive IV fluids, consider sodium bicarbonate / calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia, urgent nephrology referral  

- **Limb loss / necrotising fasciitis**  
  - Single finding: pain out of proportion, tense limb, blistering/skin necrosis (late)  
  - This is a surgical emergency: immediate orthopaedic/peripheral vascular referral, do not delay fasciotomy for imaging  

- **Crush syndrome** (if significant muscle injury)  
  - Single finding: massive CK elevation (>5000 U/L) and hyperphosphataemia  
  - Requires renal protection: maintain urine output >100 mL/hr with IV fluids, consider urinary alkalinisation
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms**  
  - Pain out of proportion to injury (the most reliable early sign)  
  - Pain on passive stretch of muscles in the affected compartment  
  - Paraesthesia / numbness (usually median/ulnar/peroneal distribution)  
  - Weakness or paralysis (late sign)  
  - Pallor, pulselessness (late, poor prognostic signs – never rely on pulses)  

- **Key exam findings**  
  - Tense, swollen, wood-like compartment on palpation  
  - Decreased two-point discrimination or sensation  
  - Pain on gentle passive flexion/extension of digits (hallmark)  
  - Capillary refill may be normal initially; do not reassure
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely**  
  - **Acute arterial occlusion** — sudden pain + cold + white + pulseless + absent Doppler signal. *Splitter:* compartment syndrome has preserved pulses early + pain on passive stretch  
  - **Deep vein thrombosis (DVT)** — warm, tender, Homan’s sign, not tense. *Splitter:* absence of pain on passive stretch  
  - **Cellulitis / necrotising fasciitis** — systemic toxicity, blistering, crepitus. *Splitter:* compartment syndrome lacks circumferential erythema and gas on XR  
  - **Neuropraxia / nerve injury** — isolated sensory/motor deficit without swelling or pain on stretch  
  - **Haemarthrosis** — joint effusion, limited range but no tense compartment  
- **Splitter**  
  - Pain on passive stretch is pathognomonic for compartment syndrome; if absent, consider other diagnoses
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour)**  
  - Full neurovascular exam both limbs — document pulse presence and sensorimotor status  
  - Doppler pulse interrogation (if concern for arterial injury)  
  - Compartment pressure measurement using Stryker needle or similar (if trained) – normal <10 mmHg; threshold for fasciotomy: >30 mmHg or Δp (diastolic BP – compartment pressure) <30 mmHg  
  - Check urine colour: dipstick for blood (myoglobin)  
  - **Never-miss test:** Compartment pressure measurement (or if unavailable, clinical diagnosis is sufficient)  

- **Bloods**  
  - Venous blood gas (VBG) / arterial blood gas (ABG): potassium, lactate, pH, HCO3  
  - Urea & electrolytes (U&E): potassium, creatinine, calcium, phosphate  
  - Creatine kinase (CK) – baseline and serial q6h  
  - Full blood count (FBC), coagulation screen, group & save (if surgery)  
  - Lactate – marker of ischaemia  

- **Imaging**  
  - **First-line:** Plain radiograph of affected limb – rule out fracture (which may cause or coexist)  
  - **Second-line:** None routinely needed; MRI or CT can suggest oedema but surgery should not be delayed for imaging  

- **Diagnostic criteria**  
  - **ΔP (difference between diastolic BP and compartment pressure)** <30 mmHg is key threshold for fasciotomy  

- **Never-miss test**  
  - **Compartment pressure measurement** – if unavailable, clinical suspicion alone warrants surgical consultation
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**  
  - Remove all circumferential dressings, splints, casts (split down to skin)  
  - Place limb at heart level (not elevated, not dependent – worsens perfusion)  
  - Give high-flow oxygen (15 L/min via non-rebreather mask)  
  - Insert two large-bore IV cannulae and start IV crystalloid (0.9% NaCl or Hartmann’s) – target urine output 1–2 mL/kg/hr  
  - **Urgent orthopaedic referral** for fasciotomy – do not delay for imaging  
  - Analgesia: IV morphine 2.5–5 mg titrated (check local BNF) [check local policy for dose escalation]  
  - If hyperkalaemia on VBG → 10 mL 10% calcium gluconate IV over 2 min + 10 units Actrapid in 50 mL 50% glucose over 15 min (call ICU/senior)  

- **Ward (first 24–72h)**  
  - Post-fasciotomy: wound management with sterile saline-soaked gauze, scheduled dressing changes  
  - Continue aggressive IV fluids (crystalloid at 200–300 mL/hr) until CK peaks and urine output adequate  
  - Serial CK, U&E, potassium, lactate q6h  
  - Monitor for compartment syndrome recurrence (second-look surgery often needed)  

- **Long-term / outpatient**  
  - Delayed primary closure or skin grafting for fasciotomy wounds after oedema resolved (typically 3–5 days)  
  - Physiotherapy and occupational therapy for range of motion and strength  
  - Consider nerve injury assessment (electromyography) if persistent deficit  

- **Default Orders (if admitted)**  
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% NaCl or Hartmann’s at 200 mL/hr initially; adjust based on urine output and CK  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous once daily (if no contraindication – check bleeding risk post-trauma)  
  - **Diet:** NBM until surgical review; then clear fluids advancing as tolerated  
  - **Observation frequency:** Obs (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, NEWS2) q1h; neurovascular checks of the limb q1h (document pulses, sensation, motor function, pain on stretch)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents**  
  - **Morphine IV** 2.5–5 mg bolus, repeat q5–10 min PRN for severe pain **[check local policy]**  
  - **Ondansetron IV** 4 mg (for nausea)  
- **Common alternatives**  
  - **Paracetamol IV** 1 g q6h (adjunct analgesia, no renal contraindication)  
  - **Ibuprofen / NSAIDs** are **contraindicated** in acute compartment syndrome due to risk of renal impairment and masking of pain  
- **Key contraindications / interactions**  
  - Avoid NSAIDs in AKI or oliguria  
  - Avoid high-dose opioids if respiratory compromise  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**  
  - Morphine – reduce dose in renal failure (accumulation of metabolites)  
  - Enoxaparin – caution if CrCl <30 mL/min (use prophylactic dose at 20 mg daily or consider UFH)  
- **Most dangerous dose error**  
  - **Giving NSAIDs (diclofenac, ibuprofen) before confirming renal function** – can precipitate AKI in rhabdomyolysis  
  - **Morphine overdose** leading to respiratory depression – ensure Naloxone available
sections.Complications:
- **Acute**  
  - Rhabdomyolysis → AKI, hyperkalaemia, cardiac arrest  
  - Irreversible nerve damage (median, ulnar, peroneal)  
  - Muscle necrosis → amputation  
  - Sepsis from necrotic tissue  
- **Chronic**  
  - Volkmann’s ischaemic contracture (flexion deformity)  
  - Chronic pain, sensory deficits, weakness  
  - Chronic compartment syndrome (rare, exertional)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** Low if recognised early (<1% with prompt fasciotomy)  
- **Limb salvage:** >95% if fasciotomy within 6 hours; >50% require amputation if >12 hours delay  
- **Functional recovery:** Good if prompt treatment; chronic contracture rate ~20% even with timely fasciotomy
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always examine the unaffected limb** – compare tone, sensation, and pain response  
- **Document hourly neurovascular checks** – include pain on passive stretch in nursing notes  
- **Check CK and urine dipstick early** – if CK >5000 U/L or dipstick positive for blood with no RBCs on microscopy → start aggressive IV fluids
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Assuming normal distal pulses rules out compartment syndrome** – pulses are present until very late (>30 mmHg pressure still allows flow)  
- **Waiting for compartment pressure measurement if clinical suspicion is high** – delay increases ischaemia time  
- **Elevating the limb above heart level** – this reduces perfusion pressure and worsens ischaemia  
- **Giving NSAIDs** – increases AKI risk and masks pain progression
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening pain, especially on moving the limb  
  - Fever, chills, or dark urine (signs of rhabdomyolysis or infection)  
  - Numbness, weakness, or loss of function in the limb  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Orthopaedic clinic in 1–2 weeks for wound review; physiotherapy referral
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (orthopaedic reg / consultant) if:**  
  - Clinical suspicion of compartment syndrome (pain on stretch, tense limb)  
  - Compartment pressure >30 mmHg or ΔP <30 mmHg  
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**  
  - Potassium >6.5 mmol/L on VBG (hyperkalaemic arrest risk)  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or pH <7.2  
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr) despite adequate fluid resuscitation  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rapid deterioration in consciousness  
- **Document all decisions and referrals**

rawMarkdown:
## Compartment Syndrome

**One-liner:** Acute limb-threatening ischaemia due to raised pressure in a closed fascial compartment; most common after fracture, requires emergency fasciotomy.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Rhabdomyolysis → acute kidney injury (AKI)**  
  - Single finding: dark/tea-coloured urine (myoglobinuria) + rising potassium >6.0 mmol/L on VBG  
  - This is a medical emergency: aggressive IV fluids, consider sodium bicarbonate / calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia, urgent nephrology referral  

- **Limb loss / necrotising fasciitis**  
  - Single finding: pain out of proportion, tense limb, blistering/skin necrosis (late)  
  - This is a surgical emergency: immediate orthopaedic/peripheral vascular referral, do not delay fasciotomy for imaging  

- **Crush syndrome** (if significant muscle injury)  
  - Single finding: massive CK elevation (>5000 U/L) and hyperphosphataemia  
  - Requires renal protection: maintain urine output >100 mL/hr with IV fluids, consider urinary alkalinisation  

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms**  
  - Pain out of proportion to injury (the most reliable early sign)  
  - Pain on passive stretch of muscles in the affected compartment  
  - Paraesthesia / numbness (usually median/ulnar/peroneal distribution)  
  - Weakness or paralysis (late sign)  
  - Pallor, pulselessness (late, poor prognostic signs – never rely on pulses)  

- **Key exam findings**  
  - Tense, swollen, wood-like compartment on palpation  
  - Decreased two-point discrimination or sensation  
  - Pain on gentle passive flexion/extension of digits (hallmark)  
  - Capillary refill may be normal initially; do not reassure  

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely**  
  - **Acute arterial occlusion** — sudden pain + cold + white + pulseless + absent Doppler signal. *Splitter:* compartment syndrome has preserved pulses early + pain on passive stretch  
  - **Deep vein thrombosis (DVT)** — warm, tender, Homan’s sign, not tense. *Splitter:* absence of pain on passive stretch  
  - **Cellulitis / necrotising fasciitis** — systemic toxicity, blistering, crepitus. *Splitter:* compartment syndrome lacks circumferential erythema and gas on XR  
  - **Neuropraxia / nerve injury** — isolated sensory/motor deficit without swelling or pain on stretch  
  - **Haemarthrosis** — joint effusion, limited range but no tense compartment  
- **Splitter**  
  - Pain on passive stretch is pathognomonic for compartment syndrome; if absent, consider other diagnoses  

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour)**  
  - Full neurovascular exam both limbs — document pulse presence and sensorimotor status  
  - Doppler pulse interrogation (if concern for arterial injury)  
  - Compartment pressure measurement using Stryker needle or similar (if trained) – normal <10 mmHg; threshold for fasciotomy: >30 mmHg or Δp (diastolic BP – compartment pressure) <30 mmHg  
  - Check urine colour: dipstick for blood (myoglobin)  
  - **Never-miss test:** Compartment pressure measurement (or if unavailable, clinical diagnosis is sufficient)  

- **Bloods**  
  - Venous blood gas (VBG) / arterial blood gas (ABG): potassium, lactate, pH, HCO3  
  - Urea & electrolytes (U&E): potassium, creatinine, calcium, phosphate  
  - Creatine kinase (CK) – baseline and serial q6h  
  - Full blood count (FBC), coagulation screen, group & save (if surgery)  
  - Lactate – marker of ischaemia  

- **Imaging**  
  - **First-line:** Plain radiograph of affected limb – rule out fracture (which may cause or coexist)  
  - **Second-line:** None routinely needed; MRI or CT can suggest oedema but surgery should not be delayed for imaging  

- **Diagnostic criteria**  
  - **ΔP (difference between diastolic BP and compartment pressure)** <30 mmHg is key threshold for fasciotomy  

- **Never-miss test**  
  - **Compartment pressure measurement** – if unavailable, clinical suspicion alone warrants surgical consultation  

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**  
  - Remove all circumferential dressings, splints, casts (split down to skin)  
  - Place limb at heart level (not elevated, not dependent – worsens perfusion)  
  - Give high-flow oxygen (15 L/min via non-rebreather mask)  
  - Insert two large-bore IV cannulae and start IV crystalloid (0.9% NaCl or Hartmann’s) – target urine output 1–2 mL/kg/hr  
  - **Urgent orthopaedic referral** for fasciotomy – do not delay for imaging  
  - Analgesia: IV morphine 2.5–5 mg titrated (check local BNF) [check local policy for dose escalation]  
  - If hyperkalaemia on VBG → 10 mL 10% calcium gluconate IV over 2 min + 10 units Actrapid in 50 mL 50% glucose over 15 min (call ICU/senior)  

- **Ward (first 24–72h)**  
  - Post-fasciotomy: wound management with sterile saline-soaked gauze, scheduled dressing changes  
  - Continue aggressive IV fluids (crystalloid at 200–300 mL/hr) until CK peaks and urine output adequate  
  - Serial CK, U&E, potassium, lactate q6h  
  - Monitor for compartment syndrome recurrence (second-look surgery often needed)  

- **Long-term / outpatient**  
  - Delayed primary closure or skin grafting for fasciotomy wounds after oedema resolved (typically 3–5 days)  
  - Physiotherapy and occupational therapy for range of motion and strength  
  - Consider nerve injury assessment (electromyography) if persistent deficit  

- **Default Orders (if admitted)**  
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% NaCl or Hartmann’s at 200 mL/hr initially; adjust based on urine output and CK  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous once daily (if no contraindication – check bleeding risk post-trauma)  
  - **Diet:** NBM until surgical review; then clear fluids advancing as tolerated  
  - **Observation frequency:** Obs (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, NEWS2) q1h; neurovascular checks of the limb q1h (document pulses, sensation, motor function, pain on stretch)  

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line agents**  
  - **Morphine IV** 2.5–5 mg bolus, repeat q5–10 min PRN for severe pain **[check local policy]**  
  - **Ondansetron IV** 4 mg (for nausea)  
- **Common alternatives**  
  - **Paracetamol IV** 1 g q6h (adjunct analgesia, no renal contraindication)  
  - **Ibuprofen / NSAIDs** are **contraindicated** in acute compartment syndrome due to risk of renal impairment and masking of pain  
- **Key contraindications / interactions**  
  - Avoid NSAIDs in AKI or oliguria  
  - Avoid high-dose opioids if respiratory compromise  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**  
  - Morphine – reduce dose in renal failure (accumulation of metabolites)  
  - Enoxaparin – caution if CrCl <30 mL/min (use prophylactic dose at 20 mg daily or consider UFH)  
- **Most dangerous dose error**  
  - **Giving NSAIDs (diclofenac, ibuprofen) before confirming renal function** – can precipitate AKI in rhabdomyolysis  
  - **Morphine overdose** leading to respiratory depression – ensure Naloxone available  

### Complications

- **Acute**  
  - Rhabdomyolysis → AKI, hyperkalaemia, cardiac arrest  
  - Irreversible nerve damage (median, ulnar, peroneal)  
  - Muscle necrosis → amputation  
  - Sepsis from necrotic tissue  
- **Chronic**  
  - Volkmann’s ischaemic contracture (flexion deformity)  
  - Chronic pain, sensory deficits, weakness  
  - Chronic compartment syndrome (rare, exertional)  

### Prognosis

- **Mortality:** Low if recognised early (<1% with prompt fasciotomy)  
- **Limb salvage:** >95% if fasciotomy within 6 hours; >50% require amputation if >12 hours delay  
- **Functional recovery:** Good if prompt treatment; chronic contracture rate ~20% even with timely fasciotomy  

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always examine the unaffected limb** – compare tone, sensation, and pain response  
- **Document hourly neurovascular checks** – include pain on passive stretch in nursing notes  
- **Check CK and urine dipstick early** – if CK >5000 U/L or dipstick positive for blood with no RBCs on microscopy → start aggressive IV fluids  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Assuming normal distal pulses rules out compartment syndrome** – pulses are present until very late (>30 mmHg pressure still allows flow)  
- **Waiting for compartment pressure measurement if clinical suspicion is high** – delay increases ischaemia time  
- **Elevating the limb above heart level** – this reduces perfusion pressure and worsens ischaemia  
- **Giving NSAIDs** – increases AKI risk and masks pain progression  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening pain, especially on moving the limb  
  - Fever, chills, or dark urine (signs of rhabdomyolysis or infection)  
  - Numbness, weakness, or loss of function in the limb  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Orthopaedic clinic in 1–2 weeks for wound review; physiotherapy referral  

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (orthopaedic reg / consultant) if:**  
  - Clinical suspicion of compartment syndrome (pain on stretch, tense limb)  
  - Compartment pressure >30 mmHg or ΔP <30 mmHg  
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**  
  - Potassium >6.5 mmol/L on VBG (hyperkalaemic arrest risk)  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or pH <7.2  
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr) despite adequate fluid resuscitation  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rapid deterioration in consciousness  
- **Document all decisions and referrals**
